content.subtitle: General rule
content.description: When the reporting verb appears in the past tense (e.g., said), the tense of the original words usually shifts back by one. This is the core principle, though a few exceptions apply. Specifically, the past perfect simple and past perfect continuous stay the same — no further backshift is required.

content.subtitle: Tense changes table
items: Direct speech: present simple → Reported speech: past simple
Direct speech: present continuous → Reported speech: past continuous
Direct speech: present perfect simple → Reported speech: past perfect simple
Direct speech: present perfect continuous → Reported speech: past perfect continuous
Direct speech: past simple → Reported speech: past perfect simple
Direct speech: past continuous → Reported speech: past perfect continuous

content.subtitle: Examples
items: present simple: Sam **doesn't play** hockey very often. → Fiona said that Sam **didn't play** hockey very often.
present continuous: I'm **winning!** → Carol shouted to us that she was **winning.**
present perfect simple: I've never **been given** a trophy before! → Paul said that he'd **never been given** a trophy before.
present perfect continuous: They've **been playing** for four hours. → She told us that they'd **been playing** for four hours.
past simple: We **lost** the match. → Finally he told us that they had **lost** the match.
past continuous: We were **winning** until half-time. → She said they **had been winning** until half-time.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When something is still true, we can change tense but we don't have to. When we are reporting a scientific or historical fact, we don't usually change tense.
examples: ✓ The article said that fishing **is/was** the most popular sport in Britain.
✓ Alan told me that Queen Victoria **hated** Gladstone, the Prime Minister.

sections.title: Reported speech: modal and semi-modal changes

content.subtitle: Modal changes
content.description: A past reporting verb (e.g., said) can also trigger changes to modals and semi-modals. However, could, would, should, ought to, had better and need remain unchanged in reported speech.

content.subtitle: Modal changes table
items: will/shall → would
can → could
must → must / had to / was/were to / should
have to → had to
don't/doesn't have to → didn't have to
mustn't → mustn't / was/were not to / shouldn't
may → might
am/is/are going to → was/were going to

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: When expressing obligation, **must** usually changes to **had to**, **was/were to** or **should**.
When expressing obligation, **mustn't** can become **mustn't**, **was/were not to** or **shouldn't**.
**Must** doesn't usually change to **had to** or **should** when we are expressing probability.

sections.title: Reported speech: pronoun, determiner, time and place changes

content.subtitle: Pronoun and determiner changes
items: my → his/her
this/that + noun → the/that
this/that + verb → it/that
these/those + noun → the/those
these/those + verb → they
verb + these/those (without object) → them

content.subtitle: Time and place changes
items: here → there
tomorrow → the following/next day, the day after
tonight → that night
yesterday → the day before, the previous day
now, at the moment → then, at that moment
next week/year/etc → the following/next week/year, the week after
last week/year/etc → the week before, the previous week
ago → before, previously, earlier

content.subtitle: What about?
content.description: We do not need to change time words/phrases when the information is still true at the moment of speaking/writing. In narratives, writers often use direct speech time words and phrases for dramatic effect.

sections.title: Reported questions, commands, requests, and reporting verbs

content.subtitle: Reported questions
content.description: We make the same tense, pronoun, and time/place changes as with reported speech. We don't use the question form or question marks.
examples: Do you want to play Monopoly? asked Cheryl. → Cheryl asked **if/whether** we wanted to play Monopoly.
What time did the match start? asked Jimmy. → Jimmy asked Andrea **what time the match had started**.

content.subtitle: Infinitive structure
content.description: The structure 'question word + infinitive' is very common with reported questions.
examples: She asked me **how to tune a piano.**

content.subtitle: Reported commands and requests
examples: Commands: 'Put the cricket bats away!' said Alex. → Alex **told me to put** the cricket bats away.
Requests: 'Would you put the cricket bats away?' asked Alex. → Alex **asked me to put** the cricket bats away.

content.subtitle: Reporting verbs
content.description: Different reporting verbs take different grammatical patterns (e.g., deny doing, deny sth, deny that…).
examples: Common reporting verbs include: accuse, agree, apologise, ask, beg, claim, command, cry, deny, explain, instruct, order, promise, refuse, reply, respond, say, shout, state, suggest, tell, whisper.

sections.title: A: Similar Meaning Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: Marcus said that he absolutely wasn't attending the conference on Friday.
items.answer: 'I'm absolutely not attending the conference on Friday,' said Marcus.

items.sentence: Rachel told Tom that he'd forgotten to lock the front door.
items.answer: 'You've forgotten to lock the front door, Tom!' said Rachel.

items.sentence: Nina admitted that she had been exaggerating when she'd said she had no part in the incident.
items.answer: 'Actually, I was exaggerating when I said I had no part in the incident,' admitted Nina.

items.sentence: Oliver told me that Claire hadn't been especially motivated when she was at college.
items.answer: 'Claire wasn't especially motivated when she was at college,' said Oliver.

items.sentence: Dylan shouted that he hadn't given his permission.
items.answer: 'I haven't given my permission!' shouted Dylan.

items.sentence: Lucy said she'd join us all at the park in twenty minutes.
items.answer: 'I'll join you all at the park in twenty minutes,' said Lucy.

items.sentence: Max's dad told him that he didn't have to finish his vegetables if he wasn't hungry.
items.answer: 'You don't have to finish your vegetables if you're not hungry, Max,' said his dad.

items.sentence: Sophia announced that she and Felix were going to launch a podcast.
items.answer: 'Felix and I are going to launch a podcast,' announced Sophia.

items.sentence: Harry asked Leila when she had moved abroad.
items.answer: 'When did you move abroad, Leila?' asked Harry.

items.sentence: Elena asked Nikos if she could use his laptop for a little while.
items.answer: 'Nikos, can I use your laptop for a little while?' asked Elena.

sections.title: B: Multiple Choice (Tense Changes)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: When I told Ms Owens that the cat **has / had** knocked my project off the desk, she wasn't impressed.
Priya told me that her brother **doesn't / didn't** drink coffee at all.
When I bumped into Leo yesterday evening, he told me that he is **/ was** planning to view a house in the morning.
Clara told me about the trivia night. She said her team **were / had been** leading in the opening rounds, but that it **all went / had all gone** wrong during the science category.
When we saw Midnight Storm, Rosa said she **has / had** never sat through a more boring film in her life!
Did Newton say that gravity **pulls / pulled** objects towards the Earth?
Emma told me on Thursday that she **has / had** just run into our old neighbour!
Victor informed everyone last week that he is **/ was** finally going back to university.
Did you say that you **do / did** want extra cheese on your pasta or not?
I told Christine last month that the library **isn't / wasn't** open on Sundays, so I have no idea why she went yesterday.

sections.title: C: Error Correction (Modal Changes)
sections.instructions: If the word or phrase in bold is correct, write OK. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Nina did mention that she probably **wouldn't be** available on Monday.
Last Monday she told us that we **don't have to** submit that task, and then on Wednesday she said **she'd made** an error.
Did you say these invoices **had to** be settled before next Friday?
Liam said he **could** ride a bicycle when he was three. Is that even possible?
Jessica told my sister that **she'd had better** hire an accountant. I think she has a point.
Before the ceremony, Patrick reminded me that **I must be** on my very best behaviour throughout. I found that rather patronising.
Chloe said that Mr Henderson **was to** have missed the deadline, but I'm not convinced. I think he simply didn't bother.
Growing up, my gran always used to tell me that **I'm not to** stay up past nine o'clock.
I was relieved when Kate told us that she **can** assist us with the decorating. If only I'd realised then how little she actually knew!
At secondary school, our PE teacher always told us that we **don't have to** warm up but we **do have to be quiet.**
items.answer: didn't have to, had to be quiet.

items.sentence: The email they sent three months ago said that they **shall** be sending a follow-up shortly. I've had nothing since.
Five years ago, the director promised that the company **will** be carbon-neutral by now. That clearly hasn't happened.

sections.title: D: Fill in the Gap (Pronouns and Determiners)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: I actually loved the jacket but Claire told me …………… clashed with the trousers I was planning to wear.
Michael told everyone in no uncertain terms that ………………… performance was a disappointment.
Why did Patrick say that …………………… brother's name is Leo? His name is Lorenzo, isn't it?
Sophie told the receptionist – quite bluntly, I felt – that ………………… wasn't what she had booked.
My dad said that …………………… headphones were far too pricey, so I left them on the shelf.
He did actually give me …………………… address but I completely forgot to save it.
Grandpa asked me to place …………………… on the shelf.
Isabelle and Theo told me that …………………… car had been towed away overnight.

sections.title: E: Fill in the Gap (Time and Place Changes)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap, based on the conversation provided.

items.sentence: Valentina asked Riku what he was working on at ………………… moment.
Riku replied that right ………………… he was just catching up on emails.
Valentina brought up a chat they had had the ………………… week.
Riku mentioned that he had attended the workshop three days ………………… .
Riku reminded Valentina that he was flying to Berlin ………………… month.
Riku told Valentina that he had been meaning to message her the day ………………… .
Valentina said she was at the studio and proposed they catch up ………………… .

sections.title: F: Similar Meaning Transformation
sections.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentence: Helena told her friends that she and Marco had visited a wonderful gallery when they were in Spain the **previous / next / current** month.
Daniel's flatmate told him she had only ironed **those / these / this** shirts the day before.
As she was heading out, Mia told her intern he **needed / needs / will need** to have all the reports submitted by the next day.
Dad told me that they **would / will / shall** see us all back there in precisely forty-five minutes from then.
Nathan said thankfully that he **didn't / doesn't / won't** have to travel to the branch that Sunday.
Eva commented to Finn that it **couldn't / can't / wasn't** be much fun having all those lorries rumbling past constantly.
Anna's grandmother said that she **would / will / can** place those on the windowsill right then.
Chris told Maya that he had told Sandra three days **before / after / later** that that proposal would need to be revised.

sections.title: G: Keyword Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Will you come with me to the exhibition next Saturday? Lucia asked Marco. go
items.answer: Lucia asked Marco if he would go with her to the exhibition the following Saturday.

items.sentence: 'Does the ferry leave at half past two?' asked Stella. whether
items.answer: Stella asked whether the ferry left at half past two.

items.sentence: 'Have you been revising all evening, Jake?' asked his mother. if
items.answer: Jake's mother asked if he had been revising all evening.

items.sentence: 'Are you all travelling together tomorrow?' Isabel asked us. whether
items.answer: Isabel asked whether we were all travelling together the next day.

items.sentence: 'Doesn't Sasha live near you any more?' asked Ben. me
items.answer: Ben asked me whether Sasha didn't live near me any more.

items.sentence: 'Shall I forward you the document about it, Laura?' asked Ross. her
items.answer: Ross asked Laura if he should forward her the document about it.

items.sentence: 'Shall I give you a hand with the gardening, Yuki?' said Callum. offered
items.answer: Callum offered to give Yuki a hand with the gardening.

items.sentence: 'Do we really have to leave the hotel right now?' Zara asked her parents. did
items.answer: Zara asked her parents if they really did have to leave the hotel right then.

sections.title: H: Multiple Choice (Question Word Order)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Amara didn't dare ask Freya where **had she / she had** gone the night before.
I was curious to ask why **did Marcus decide / Marcus decided** to transfer to the other department so suddenly.
We did ask Nina who **she had / had she** nominated, but she refused to say.
You won't believe it — Josh actually asked me how **to / could he** attach a file to an email!
Nobody has asked me why **didn't I / I didn't** show up at the meeting on Thursday.
I'm going to ask them what exactly **did they mean / they meant** by that comment.
Go and ask someone at the desk who **should we / to** address the complaint to.
Mia and Jack didn't even ask me how **the renovation was / was the renovation** progressing.

sections.title: I: Verb Patterns (Infinitive/Gerund)
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: You'd better ask the caretaker how ………………… into the sports hall after hours.
It was really thoughtful of them to ask whether I ………………………… to join them for dinner.
I know you asked me ……………………………… the recycling now, but I'll only be a moment, I promise.
Why did the manager instruct her team ……………………… without checking the data first?
I would ask you …………………………… judgement until you've heard the full story.
I was rather taken aback when Owen came out and asked me if I ……………………… that particular route before.
I dislike asking you ……………………… on a public holiday, but there really is no other option.
You should have asked the tailor which fabric ……………………….. He would definitely know.
It was quite amusing when you were asked by the support technician how ……………………… the printer on.
He very graciously asked me if I ……………………… carrot cake and mentioned there was a cheesecake option too.
Pop and ask your sister ……………………… us some tea, will you?
I'm afraid we've been advised …………………………… to journalists under any circumstances.
Do you deny ………………… this with the accused on the morning in question?
Why did you refuse ………………… the inspector's questions? It really doesn't reflect well on you.
It's high time Mr Walsh apologised to me ………………… me unreliable in front of the whole team.
He may claim ………………… a black belt, but no one has ever seen him in action.
The children suggested ………………… sushi for dinner tonight. Shall we?
I do agree there ………………… straightforward answer to this dilemma.
Leo responded ………………… that at least he had always been honest with his colleagues.
I'm begging ………………… .

sections.title: K: Fill in the Gap (Reporting Verbs in Context)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The next time a colleague asks me (1) ...... my goals are for the year, or, come to think of it, (2) ...... I even have any goals, I'm genuinely tempted to refuse (3) ...... respond.
Perhaps instead I'll flip the conversation and ask them to (4) ...... me what their own goals look like.
No doubt they'll claim to (5) ...... deeply passionate about disrupting industries or transforming workplace culture, but I seriously doubt it.
I don't think we should deny (6) ...... any longer: the annual goal-setting exercise has become entirely meaningless.
I would like to suggest (7) ...... , going forward, the entire process is replaced with something more genuine.
You may accuse me (8) ...... being overly cynical, but I firmly believe the evidence is on my side.
Ask any professional — junior or senior, for that matter — how they (9) ...... their most productive hours each day.
They'll respond (10) ...... a string of buzzwords — synergising, upskilling, pivoting, networking, disrupting, scaling — none of which constitutes a meaningful goal.
